---
title: "Arizona Porphyry Cu (v4)"
subtitle: "Cox-Singer-cleaned porphyry-Cu prospectivity over the southeastern Arizona belt"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import json
from pathlib import Path

import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from ai_minerals.regions.arizona import ARIZONA

DATA_DERIVED = Path("/home/sky/src/learning/ai-minerals/data/derived")
AZ_DERIVED = DATA_DERIVED / "arizona"
FEATURES = DATA_DERIVED / "features_arizona_500m.parquet"
METRICS = AZ_DERIVED / "path3_decomposed_metrics.json"
```

## Summary

Same modeling pipeline as Mother Lode v3 and Tanacross v1, applied to
the densest Laramide porphyry-copper belt in the United States. The
goal of this region is reproducibility and cross-region comparison —
running the same methodology on a third geologically- and
commodity-distinct AOI to test whether the within-region performance
of the public-data feature stack holds up when neither the labels
nor the geology are the ones the pipeline was first developed
against.

The headline finding: the standard Random Forest on the
seventy-seven-column feature stack captures **23.0 percent of
held-out porphyry-Cu positives in its top 1 percent of cells**
(enrichment factor 23 over random sampling), and **51.3 percent in
its top 5 percent**. Same model class, different region, different
deposit type, comparable lift to the earlier Mother Lode and
Tanacross numbers. The result also feeds the four-region cross-
methodology experiment (Curnamona REE, Tanacross porphyry-Cu,
Arizona porphyry-Cu, Lawley continental MVT) that gets its own
notebook.

## AOI and deposit type

The southeastern Arizona porphyry belt covers a rectangle from
$-110.4°$ to $-109.0°$ W and $32.0°$ to $33.7°$ N — roughly 150 km
east-west by 190 km north-south, total area about 28,500 km². The
AOI contains five major porphyry-Cu districts: Globe-Miami, Pima,
Ray, Safford, and Morenci, including world-class deposits like
Morenci, Ray, Sierrita, Pinto Valley, and Resolution.

The deposit type is Cox-Singer model 17 (porphyry Cu), 21a (porphyry
Cu-Mo), and 20c (porphyry Cu, low-fluorine subtype). We exclude
21b (porphyry Mo low-F) from the positive label set — it's a
Mo-dominant deposit type with different host rocks and different
exploration targeting. After the Cox-Singer commodity-and-deposit-
type filter against MRDS, the AOI contains **191 porphyry-Cu
positive cells** out of 98,828 total at 500 m resolution.

```{python}
#| label: aoi-map
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Arizona AOI with 191 porphyry-Cu positives (red) on a base of any-mineral-occurrence cells (gray)."
df = pd.read_parquet(FEATURES, columns=['x', 'y', 'is_porphyry_cu', 'any_mineral_occurrence'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 8))
neg = df[df['is_porphyry_cu'] == 0]
any_occ = df[(df['is_porphyry_cu'] == 0) & (df['any_mineral_occurrence'] == 1)]
pos = df[df['is_porphyry_cu'] == 1]
ax.scatter(neg['x'], neg['y'], s=0.3, c='#e8e8e8', alpha=0.4, label='AOI cells')
ax.scatter(any_occ['x'], any_occ['y'], s=2, c='#888', alpha=0.6, label='any mineral occurrence')
ax.scatter(pos['x'], pos['y'], s=25, c='#cc0033', alpha=0.85, label='porphyry-Cu positive', edgecolors='black', linewidths=0.3)
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_title("Arizona SE porphyry belt, 191 Cox-Singer-cleaned porphyry-Cu positives")
ax.set_xlabel("CA Albers x (m)")
ax.set_ylabel("CA Albers y (m)")
ax.legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The positive cells cluster along the Laramide-age intrusive arc,
running roughly NW-SE through the southern part of the AOI. The
visible alignment of positives with the major district centers is
expected: this is one of the most extensively explored porphyry-Cu
belts in the world, and the spatial pattern of known deposits
reflects both the geological substrate (the Laramide arc) and a
century of exploration history.

## Why this region — methodological choice

Arizona's southeastern porphyry belt was picked as the third
regional AOI for two reasons:

The first reason is **commodity relevance**. Porphyry copper is the
primary deposit type driving the AI-driven mineral-exploration
industry's current targeting work. Running the same methodology on
a porphyry-Cu region makes the within-region numbers directly
relevant to that workflow.

The second reason is **methodological control**. Tanacross v1 was a
mixed-metallogenic AOI (porphyry-Cu, distal porphyry, polymetallic
veins all in the cleaned label set), and Mother Lode v3 was orogenic
Au. Arizona is a single-event, single-deposit-type AOI: every
positive in the 191-cell label set is a Laramide porphyry-Cu deposit
formed in a narrow 66-74 Ma window in a single tectonic setting.
This homogeneous label set was the cleanest test of whether the
DEEP-SEAM-class deep-anomaly-detection architectures would beat
classical tree-based methods when the deposit population is
maximally homogeneous. The cross-region experiment writeup
documents the result; the v4 notebook here presents the within-region
prospectivity numbers that the cross-region work was built on top of.

## Data layers

Same public-data feature stack as Mother Lode and Tanacross,
adapted to Arizona's geology:

- **USGS magnetic + Bouguer gravity + isostatic gravity** national
  grids, clipped to the AOI. Porphyry-Cu intrusive centers typically
  produce a positive magnetic anomaly (from magnetite in the host
  intrusion) and a moderate negative residual gravity anomaly (from
  the alteration halo around the intrusion).
- **Four magnetic derivatives** (1VD, HGM, analytic signal, tilt)
  computed from the residual TI grid via scipy convolution kernels.
  These are the standard structural-edge enhancement layers used
  in porphyry-Cu exploration.
- **SGMC State Geologic Map Compilation** for canonical 33-class
  lithology plus the MAJOR1/2/3 fine-grained lithology columns
  (~hundreds of distinct intrusive-unit names).
- **NGDB stream-sediment chemistry** for the eleven-element porphyry-
  Cu pathfinder suite: Cu, Mo, Pb, Zn, Au, Ag, As, Sb, W, Bi, Te.
  Four statistics per element per cell (mean, max, count, has-data
  flag) within a 5 km radius.
- **Distance to mapped fault traces** from SGMC structural lines.
- **30 m DEM** with derived slope and terrain-roughness products.
- **Cox-Singer-cleaned porphyry-Cu positive labels** from MRDS.

Sentinel-2 spectral indices were paused for this region due to
upstream data-fetcher reliability issues during the build window;
they would normally contribute iron-oxide and clay-alteration
proxies for the (typically well-exposed) Arizona desert
intrusives.

```{python}
#| label: feature-stack
#| code-fold: true
df = pd.read_parquet(FEATURES)
print(f"Feature frame: {df.shape[0]:,} cells x {df.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Cells inside AOI:     {len(df):,}")
print(f"Porphyry-Cu positives: {int(df['is_porphyry_cu'].sum()):,}")
print(f"Any-occurrence cells:  {int(df['any_mineral_occurrence'].sum()):,}")
```

```{python}
#| label: geophysics-grid
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Four geophysical layers across the Arizona AOI: residual magnetic intensity, Bouguer gravity, isostatic-residual gravity, and magnetic 1st-vertical-derivative. Porphyry-Cu districts (red) align with magnetic-high / gravity-low intersections, which is the canonical porphyry-Cu signature."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(13, 11))
df_sub = df.sample(n=min(30000, len(df)), random_state=42)
pos = df[df['is_porphyry_cu'] == 1]

for ax, col, title in [
    (axes[0, 0], 'magnetic', 'Residual magnetic intensity (nT)'),
    (axes[0, 1], 'gravity', 'Bouguer gravity (mGal)'),
    (axes[1, 0], 'gravity_isostatic', 'Isostatic-residual gravity (mGal)'),
    (axes[1, 1], 'magnetic_1vd', 'Magnetic 1st vertical derivative'),
]:
    sc = ax.scatter(df_sub['x'], df_sub['y'], c=df_sub[col], s=1.5, alpha=0.6,
                    cmap='RdBu_r', rasterized=True)
    ax.scatter(pos['x'], pos['y'], s=20, c='#cc0033', alpha=0.9,
               edgecolors='black', linewidths=0.4, label='porphyry-Cu')
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, fraction=0.04, pad=0.02)
axes[0, 0].legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: geochem-grid
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Four NGDB stream-sediment pathfinder elements across the AOI: Cu, Mo, As, and Sb (5-km radius mean, log10 scale where available). Porphyry-Cu positives (red) align with the high-Cu / high-Mo intersections, the headline pathfinder columns."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(13, 11))

for ax, col, title in [
    (axes[0, 0], 'cu_mean_5km', 'NGDB Cu (mean within 5 km)'),
    (axes[0, 1], 'mo_mean_5km', 'NGDB Mo (mean within 5 km)'),
    (axes[1, 0], 'as_mean_5km', 'NGDB As (mean within 5 km)'),
    (axes[1, 1], 'sb_mean_5km', 'NGDB Sb (mean within 5 km)'),
]:
    vals = df_sub[col].copy()
    vals = np.log10(np.where(vals > 0, vals, np.nan))
    sc = ax.scatter(df_sub['x'], df_sub['y'], c=vals, s=1.5, alpha=0.6,
                    cmap='viridis', rasterized=True)
    ax.scatter(pos['x'], pos['y'], s=20, c='#cc0033', alpha=0.9,
               edgecolors='black', linewidths=0.4, label='porphyry-Cu')
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, fraction=0.04, pad=0.02)
axes[0, 0].legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Methodology — the four-cell controlled experiment

Arizona was the third AOI in a four-region experiment whose primary
goal was to test the transferability of DEEP-SEAM-class deep-anomaly-
detection architectures across different regional MPM contexts. The
four-cell design factors model architecture against data-preprocessing
choice:

|   | DevNet (DEEP-SEAM architecture) | Random Forest (our baseline) |
|---|---|---|
| **DEEP-SEAM data style** (ILR-PCA on geochem + GLCM textural features on geophysics) | Cell A | Cell B |
| **Our data style** (raw 77-column feature stack, no decomposition) | Cell C | Cell D |

All four cells are scored under leak-free out-of-fold spatial-block
cross-validation with 20-km blocks. The same train / test partition
is used across all four cells, so the only thing that varies between
cells is the model architecture or the data preprocessing.

```{python}
#| label: oof-table
#| code-fold: true
metrics = json.loads(METRICS.read_text())
rows = []
for cell, friendly in [
    ('RF_raw',           '(D) RF on raw features'),
    ('RF_decomposed',    '(B) RF on DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessed features'),
    ('DevNet_raw',       '(C) DevNet on raw features'),
    ('DevNet_decomposed',  '(A) DevNet on DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessed features'),
]:
    cap = metrics[cell]["capture_at_top_k"]
    rows.append({
        'Cell':            friendly,
        'Top 1%':          f"{cap['top_1_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}% ({cap['top_1_pct']['captured']}/191)",
        'Top 2%':          f"{cap['top_2_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%",
        'Top 5%':          f"{cap['top_5_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%",
        'Top 10%':         f"{cap['top_10_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%",
        'Top 30%':         f"{cap['top_30_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%",
    })
pd.DataFrame(rows)
```

The table reads as out-of-fold capture rates: the fraction of
held-out positive cells that fall inside the top k percent of cells
the model ranks most prospective. The factor 23x at top-1 percent
for cell D (RF on raw features) is the canonical reporting metric
for regional MPM: the top 1 percent of pixels by predicted
probability contains 23 times the density of real porphyry-Cu
deposits that random sampling would produce.

## Headline result and cross-region comparison

```{python}
#| label: capture-curves
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Capture curves for the four cells of the Arizona experiment. The diagonal is random selection. RF on raw features (orange, top) is the leader; the DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessing helps DevNet a little but hurts RF."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
percents = [1, 2, 5, 10, 30]
x = np.array(percents) / 100

for cell, color, label in [
    ('RF_raw',          '#cc6600', '(D) RF on raw features'),
    ('RF_decomposed',   '#ff9933', '(B) RF on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
    ('DevNet_raw',      '#003366', '(C) DevNet on raw features'),
    ('DevNet_decomposed', '#3366aa', '(A) DevNet on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
]:
    cap = metrics[cell]['capture_at_top_k']
    ys = [cap[f'top_{p}_pct']['rate'] for p in percents]
    ax.plot(x, ys, '-o', color=color, label=label, lw=2, markersize=7)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], '--', color='#888', label='random', lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("Fraction of area flagged (top k%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Fraction of held-out porphyry-Cu positives captured")
ax.set_xscale('log'); ax.set_yscale('linear')
ax.set_xlim(0.008, 0.5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=9, framealpha=0.95)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_title("Arizona porphyry-Cu, four-cell OOF capture (191 positives, 20 km spatial blocks)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The standout numbers are at the top of the curve. RF on raw
features puts 23 percent of held-out positives in the top 1 percent
of cells; DevNet on raw features puts 8.9 percent there. The
DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessing helps DevNet substantially (it goes
from 8.9 to 13.6 percent at top-1) but hurts RF (it goes from 23.0
to 14.7 percent at top-1). That last result confirms a pattern we
observed on Tanacross as well: the published DEEP-SEAM preprocessing
pipeline, while genuinely helpful for the deviation-network
architecture it was tuned with, actively damages classical tree-
based models because the principal-components projection throws away
multivariate information the trees would otherwise exploit.

The cross-region table puts Arizona alongside the other regions in
the methodology experiment:

| Region | Positives | RF raw top-1 | DevNet raw top-1 | RF decomposed top-1 | DevNet decomposed top-1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Curnamona REE (LOO) | 7 | 0% | 43% | — | — |
| Tanacross (OOF) | 45 | 22.2% | 13.3% | 15.6% | 13.3% |
| **Arizona (OOF)** | **191** | **23.0%** | **8.9%** | **14.7%** | **13.6%** |

The Arizona number sits right alongside the Tanacross number for RF
on raw features, which is the reproducibility claim the regional
pipeline is meant to support. The DEEP-SEAM-vs-RF gap is wider on
Arizona (14 pp) than on Tanacross (9 pp), and both regions are
inverted relative to Curnamona — where DevNet beats RF — by
substantial margins. That inversion is the cross-region experiment's
central finding; the standalone four-cell notebook works through it.

## What this does and doesn't establish

The Arizona v4 work establishes three things:

- **The within-region prospectivity numbers reproduce.** The
  RF-on-raw-features result at top-1 percent capture (23.0 percent,
  enrichment factor 23) sits in the same regime as Mother Lode v3
  (top-5 percent capture 19.5 percent) and Tanacross v1 (top-1
  percent capture 22.2 percent). Three regions, three deposit types,
  one consistent methodology, comparable numerical results.

- **The DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessing is not a free win.** Applying
  the published ILR-PCA + GLCM pipeline to Arizona's raw features
  helps DevNet (8.9 → 13.6 percent at top-1) but hurts RF (23.0 →
  14.7 percent). The preprocessing reshapes the data into a form
  that fits the deviation-loss assumption, at the cost of
  information that the gradient-boosted-trees / random-forest
  family otherwise uses. This is the second region (after Tanacross)
  where the pattern holds in the same direction.

- **A homogeneous deposit population does not rescue the DEEP-SEAM
  architecture.** The hypothesis going into the Arizona experiment
  was that Tanacross's mixed metallogenic history (porphyry-Cu plus
  distal porphyry plus polymetallic veins, 100+ Myr of history) was
  obscuring the deviation network's signal — that a cleaner,
  single-event, single-deposit-type AOI would let the architecture
  win. Arizona's southeastern porphyry belt is exactly that
  cleaner AOI: single tectonic context, single 66-74 Ma event,
  single deposit type. DevNet still underperformed RF by 14
  percentage points at top-1. **Deposit homogeneity is not the gap.**

The Arizona v4 work does **not** establish:

- That the v4 model would generalize to other porphyry-Cu belts.
  We did not run a cross-region transfer test from Arizona to a
  second porphyry-Cu region (BCGT or the Andes). The within-Arizona
  numbers are the ceiling on what this region's data can do; the
  cross-region transfer test is a separate experiment.

- That the published Cox-Singer commodity-and-deposit-type
  filtering produces a strictly correct positive label set. The 191
  positives include some uncertainty from MRDS's free-text
  classification.

- A complete picture of feature attribution. SHAP attribution on the
  Arizona fit is computed via the same harness used on Mother Lode
  v3.1 and Tanacross v1 (see those notebooks for the in-detail
  SHAP-by-feature treatment); the geological interpretation specific
  to Arizona's porphyry-Cu pathfinder ranking is a separate writeup.

## Files and reproduction

- Region config: [src/ai_minerals/regions/arizona.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/src/ai_minerals/regions/arizona.py)
- Feature frame: [data/derived/features_arizona_500m.parquet](../../data/derived/features_arizona_500m.parquet)
- Decomposed feature frame: [data/derived/features_arizona_500m_decomposed.parquet](../../data/derived/features_arizona_500m_decomposed.parquet)
- 4-cell OOF metrics: [data/derived/arizona/path3_decomposed_metrics.json](../../data/derived/arizona/path3_decomposed_metrics.json)
- Build scripts: [arizona_fetch_all.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/arizona/fetch_all.py), [arizona_features.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/arizona/features.py), [arizona_decompose_features.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/arizona/decompose_features.py), [arizona_oof_comparison.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/arizona/oof_comparison.py)
- Companion notebook: [cross-region four-cell deep dive](../cross_region/four_cell.qmd)

## Sources

- Cox, D. P. and Singer, D. A. (eds), 1986. *Mineral deposit models*. USGS Bulletin 1693.
- Singer, D. A. and Berger, V. I., 2007. *Porphyry copper deposit models*. USGS Scientific Investigations Report 2007-5202.
- USGS, gridded aeromagnetic + Bouguer gravity + isostatic-residual gravity for the conterminous United States.
- USGS, National Geochemical Database (NGDB) stream-sediment chemistry.
- USGS, State Geologic Map Compilation (SGMC).
- USGS, Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS).
- USGS, 1 arc-second digital elevation model.
- Luo, Z. et al. 2026. DEEP-SEAM: an explainable semi-supervised deep learning framework for mineral prospectivity mapping. *Geosci. Model Dev.* 19, 2593.
- Pang, G. et al. 2019. Deep Anomaly Detection with Deviation Networks. arXiv:1911.08623.
